did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used AI/ML models, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), MobileNet, and Support Vector Machines (SVM), to classify skin lesions as malignant or benign for early skin cancer detection. The CNN model achieved 86% accuracy on the Kaggle dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a mobile application (SkinCheck) for skin cancer detection. It details a specific methodology involving data acquisition from the Kaggle dataset, preprocessing, and the implementation and comparison of three AI/ML models (CNN, SVM, MobileNet). The paper reports original results, including accuracy metrics (86% for CNN) and performance comparisons, which classifies it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: SkinCheck: A Lightweight CNN-Powered Mobile App for Early Detection of Skin Cancer
journal: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
brief_summary: This study developed SkinCheck, a lightweight mobile application for the early detection of skin cancer using Computer-Aided Diagnosis. The app utilizes three AI/ML models—Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and MobileNet—to classify skin lesions as malignant or benign. The models were trained and evaluated on the Kaggle Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign dataset, which consists of 3,297 images scaled to 224x224 pixels. The evaluation compared the performance of these architectures based on accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. The CNN model achieved the highest accuracy of 86%, outperforming MobileNet (83.6%) and SVM (77%). The findings suggest that CNN is superior for this task, offering a potential tool for improving early skin cancer detection outcomes.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study classifies images into malignant and benign categories using the Kaggle Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign dataset. Melanoma is mentioned in the introduction as a prevalent and aggressive variant of skin cancer, but the experimental classification task is binary (malignant vs. benign).
The study classifies images into malignant and benign categories using the Kaggle Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign dataset. The dataset consists of 3,297 images of skin moles.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into malignant or benign categories
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'classify skin cancer from outlier lesion images' and 'classify inspected images as cancerous or benign'. The models (CNN, SVM, MobileNet) predict discrete diagnostic labels (malignant vs. benign) using the Kaggle Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign dataset. Performance is measured using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score for these discrete classes.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign
datasets_used.description: A subset of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive curated by the International Society for Digital Imaging of the Skin (ISDIS). It consists of 3,297 images of skin moles scaled to 224x224 pixels.

use.training: Used for training the CNN, SVM, and MobileNet models. The dataset was divided into a training set and a test set using an 80:20 ratio.
use.validation: The test set portion (20%) was used for validation and testing purposes.
use.testing: Used for testing the performance of the CNN, SVM, and MobileNet models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between CNN, SVM, and MobileNet were based on accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics without statistical significance testing.

CNN.notes: Outperformed SVM and MobileNet in accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, training time, and model file size.

SVM.notes: Lower recall and f1-score for malignant data (class 1) compared to benign data.

MobileNet.notes: Used with PIC extension; Adam optimizer, learning rate 0.001, batch size 100, 20 epochs.

Kaggle Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign.notes: The CNN model achieved the highest accuracy of 86%, outperforming MobileNet (83.6%) and SVM (77%). The study evaluated precision, recall, and F1-score for CNN and SVM. MobileNet accuracy was reported as approximately 83.6%.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and MobileNet
architecture_used.pretraining: MobileNet was pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset. The CNN architecture was derived from a previous ensemble CNN development [6].

CNN.layers: Three convolutional layers and three fully connected layers
CNN.source: Derived from ensemble CNN development in reference [6]

MobileNet.architecture_type: Streamlined architecture using depth-wise separable convolutions

SVM.notes: Hyperparameters not explicitly detailed beyond kernel and C parameter; used in binary classifier combination

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization (scaling image pixel data to have zero mean and unit variance)
conclusions: The SkinCheck mobile application successfully utilizes a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify skin lesions as malignant or benign with 86% accuracy.
The CNN model outperformed Support Vector Machine (SVM) and MobileNet architectures in terms of accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, training time, and model file size.

The high specificity of the CNN model indicates its ability to accurately classify malignant cases, while its sensitivity, though higher than SVM and MobileNet, still has room for improvement to ensure no skin lesions are overlooked.
The study demonstrates that lightweight AI models can be effectively integrated into mobile applications for early skin cancer detection.
Future work is required to adapt the application for Apple iPhones, enhance the user interface and experience, and conduct further pilot studies to test usability.
The current implementation is limited to Android devices and relies on the Kaggle Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign dataset, suggesting a need for broader validation.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the Kaggle Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign dataset (a subset of ISIC), which may introduce dataset bias and limits generalizability to broader clinical populations.
No external validation was performed; the model was only tested on the held-out 20% of the Kaggle dataset.
The application is currently limited to Android devices; iOS compatibility is identified as pending future work.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was reported to support the performance differences between models.
The CNN architecture was derived from a previous study [6] rather than being newly designed, and hyperparameter tuning was limited (fixed epochs/learning rate).
Data augmentation techniques were not employed, which may limit the model's robustness to variations in real-world imaging conditions.
The binary classification task (malignant vs. benign) does not distinguish between specific types of skin cancer (e.g., melanoma vs. non-melanoma), potentially reducing clinical specificity.
The paper acknowledges that sensitivity could be improved to ensure no skin lesions are overlooked, indicating a potential risk of false negatives in early detection.
